coredata.dc:title: Biomass derived carbon quantum dots as potential tools for sustainable environmental remediation and eco-friendly food packaging

coredata.dc:description: A sustainable solution to the rampant environmental pollution is vital to the improvement of quality of life on earth. The utilization of renewable resources and waste materials for environmental remediation promotes a circular economy. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), a category of novel carbon nanoparticles, carry tremendous prospects for a wide range of applications due to their extraordinary physical and optical properties. They can be easily synthesized through cost-effective routes from a variety of biomass sources, further helping in more efficient management of different agro-industrial waste materials. Recently, the attention has shifted to the use of biomass-derived CQDs for environmental pollution remediation, with a greater emphasis on contaminated water resource treatment and the fabrication of eco-friendly food packaging materials. They have been studied for environmental applications including photocatalytic degradation, sensing, forward osmosis and adsorption. In addition, the use of CQDs as nanofillers in biopolymer nanocomposites enhance the mechanical, antimicrobial and antioxidant properties of biodegradable food packaging. They also act as biomass derived UV adsorbents that slow down food degradation through photooxidation. This review aims to provide an insight into the synthesis of CQDs from different biomass sources, their subsequent surface modification and application in environmental remediation and biodegradable packaging applications. An up-to date literature regarding the same has been presented in this review.

head.ce:title: Biomass derived carbon quantum dots as potential tools for sustainable environmental remediation and eco-friendly food packaging

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, School of Advanced Sciences, Vellore Institute of Technology, Vellore, Tamil Nadu 632014, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, School of Advanced Sciences, Vellore Institute of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, School of Advanced Sciences, Vellore Institute of technology, Vellore, 632014, Tamil Nadu, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A sustainable solution to the rampant environmental pollution is vital to the improvement of quality of life on earth. The utilization of renewable resources and waste materials for environmental remediation promotes a circular economy. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), a category of novel carbon nanoparticles, carry tremendous prospects for a wide range of applications due to their extraordinary physical and optical properties. They can be easily synthesized through cost-effective routes from a variety of biomass sources, further helping in more efficient management of different agro-industrial waste materials. Recently, the attention has shifted to the use of biomass-derived CQDs for environmental pollution remediation, with a greater emphasis on contaminated water resource treatment and the fabrication of eco-friendly food packaging materials. They have been studied for environmental applications including photocatalytic degradation, sensing, forward osmosis and adsorption. In addition, the use of CQDs as nanofillers in biopolymer nanocomposites enhance the mechanical, antimicrobial and antioxidant properties of biodegradable food packaging. They also act as biomass derived UV adsorbents that slow down food degradation through photooxidation. This review aims to provide an insight into the synthesis of CQDs from different biomass sources, their subsequent surface modification and application in environmental remediation and biodegradable packaging applications. An up-to date literature regarding the same has been presented in this review.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: In the recent past, with industrialization and economic development, environmental pollution has been on the rise. Depletion of natural resources along with the release of toxic effluents into the environment have worsened the situation, hindering sustainable development. Water and soil contamination are issues of great concern and require immediate attention. The major environmental contaminants include heavy metals, pharmaceuticals, personal care products, dyes, pesticides and other industrial effluents. Heavy metals released into the water sources are toxic and undergo bio-accumulation. Exposure to these heavy metals can cause serious health issues including mutations [1]. There are other chemicals like bisphenol A (BPA), belonging to the category of endocrine disrupting chemicals (EDCs) known to cause many adverse health effects in humans [2]. The use of organochlorine compounds as pesticides has helped the agricultural sector with improved yield but has also led to environmental degradation as pesticides are highly toxic with a persistent nature and tend to accumulate over time [3]. A different category of pollutants are organic dyes released to the environment from industries like cosmetics, paper and plastic manufacturing. They are toxic, non-biodegradable and water soluble, making them highly problematic to the environment [4,5]. All of these harmful chemicals released into the environment pose a threat to different forms of life. To control and keep environmental pollution in check have thus become a need of the hour. Development of simple, rapid, scalable and most importantly sustainable methods for the same has been a challenge and a lot of work is being conducted with this aim as well. A greater obstacle to sustainable development is the widespread usage of plastic packaging materials. With the ever-growing food industry, there is soaring plastic consumption globally. The conventional petroleum derived packaging materials are non-biodegradable and are thrown out, most of the times after a single use into the environment, where these materials undergo accumulation without well planned waste management strategies. The materials used for packaging food products are also contaminated by different organic compounds making it difficult to recycle these. Biopolymer based food packaging has therefore been the focal point recently due to their renewable and eco-friendly nature. However, the high cost and poor mechanical properties restrict the use of these materials. Efforts are being made to improve their properties through blending with other polymers and by the addition of different organic and inorganic fillers. Nanomaterials with their unique and fascinating properties are an important class of materials with great capacity for use as nanofillers in food packaging and for controlling environmental pollution. Among these, carbon nanomaterials like carbon nanotubes (CNTs), graphene quantum dots (GQDs), carbon quantum dots (CQDs), carbon nanofibers (CNFs), carbon nanospheres (CNSs), nano-biochar (NBC) have drawn a lot of attention for their ability to upgrade the properties of biopolymer packaging and to carry out effective environmental remediation [6–10]. All of these materials do possess inherent properties that enable their use as fillers in food packaging and for the remediation of environmental pollution. However, there is a need for more such materials synthesized from biomass sources offering simple synthesis, easily tunable properties, efficient, rapid, selective, on-site detection and degradation of environmental pollutants and as nanofillers with good antimicrobial, antioxidant, UV shielding ability that also improve the mechanical, barrier and thermal properties of biopolymer-based packaging materials. The category of carbon nanomaterials that cater to all of these above-mentioned requirements is CQDs.

CQDs, discovered in 2004 are carbon nanoparticles that have their sizes around 10 nm and exhibit quantum confinement effect making them highly fluorescent. Compared to other fluorescent quantum dots, they exhibit better properties like broad excitation spectra and tunable emission spectra [11,12]. They possess a quasi-spherical morphology with nitrogen or oxygen rich surface functional groups and a multilayered graphene core structure [13]. Their properties can be altered by changing their size and shape using different synthesis strategies and precursors. By controlling the temperature and duration of synthesis, CQDs of different geometric shapes and sizes can be prepared [14]. Their high quantum yield and tunable optical properties make them an important class of zero dimensional nanomaterials with wide range of applications ranging from bioimaging to catalysis. Optical absorption is a typical phenomenon exhibited by CQDs under UV irradiation and the different electronic transitions like π–π* and n–π* transitions are responsible for the peaks obtained in the absorption spectra of the CQD. CQDs also emit fluorescence over different wavelengths depending on the synthesis conditions. The dependence of emission wavelength and intensity on the excitation wavelength is a property unique to CQDs. The exact mechanism behind the fluorescence of CQDs is still unknown and is proposed that the size of the CQDs and the quantum confinement effect are responsible for the different colour emissions. Surface states are another factor that probably determine the emission of the CQDs but is very vaguely understood due to their diversity [15,16]. In order to determine the optical properties of the CQD, UV-Vis and photoluminescence (PL) spectra are studied in detail.

CQDs can be used to tackle environmental pollution as they are non-toxic and biocompatible with good photostability [12,17]. They also offer other advantages like facile surface functionalization with a variety of functional groups, chemical inertness, simple and economically feasible production and selectivity against their target [2,10]. CQDs also have conjugated structures that help them bind to those organic pollutants with electron rich benzene rings to perform enhanced photocatalysis and nanocomposites based on CQDs have been reported in the literature to effectively remove organic environmental pollutants [18]. CQDs are majorly composed of the elements C, H and O and can be synthesized from a variety of precursors both organic and inorganic [14]. CQDs synthesized from different plant extracts also tend to show good antimicrobial, antioxidant and UV shielding properties making them a suitable choice for cost-effective, non-toxic nanofillers in different polymer nanocomposites. CQDs derived from biomass have drawn a lot of attention in the recent past due to the added advantage of agricultural waste management made possible through the approach [19]. Preparation of CQDs from natural biomass sources offer low cost, simple and green routes for sustainable synthesis of useful materials. A variety of biomass sources including agricultural wastes, plant extracts, fruit peels, biopolymers, cyanobacteria have been used as cheap precursors for the same [20–24]. CQDs that also exhibit a linear relationship between fluorescence intensity and temperature have been synthesized from biomass sources [25]. Thus, compared to the other carbon nanomaterials CQDs possess superior properties like good dispersibility in water and organic solvents, unique and tunable optical properties, photostability and exhibit different intermolecular interactions enabling their use in sensing of different environmental pollutants [26–29]. Their non-toxic, antimicrobial, antioxidant and UV protectant nature can also be utilized in maintaining freshness of food materials and extending their shelf life.

A preliminary survey of the existing literature reveals that review articles focusing on the synthesis, surface doping and applications of biomass derived CQDs have been published before. A review paper published in 2020 discussed in detail the synthesis and application of CQDs as photocatalytic materials used for degradation of environmental contaminants with special emphasis on the mechanism of photocatalytic degradation [30]. In another review paper published in the same year, the utilization of CQDs in wastewater treatment is comprehensively investigated by Rani et al., [17]. Perumal et al., reviewed the synthesis of CQDs from biomass sources through the hydrothermal route, its optical properties and application in bioimaging, metal sensing and fluorescent inks [31]. A review article published in 2023 discussed toxic metal sensing by CQDs and the possible mechanisms for the same [32]. A paper published by Moradi et al., in 2021 discusses the use of CQDs derived from microorganisms and food by products for packaging applications [33]. Given the novelty and relevance of the already published review articles in this area, there is still a lack of work on the sensing of other major environmental pollutants by biomass sourced CQDs, with most of the review articles focusing only on toxic metal sensing. Previous reviews have focused on the synthesis, surface doping and applications of CQDs derived from biomass sources, but haven’t addressed in detail the role of CQDs in promoting sustainability through biodegradable packaging. Table 1 gives a comparison of the published reviews with this work. Papers with greater similarity with this work have been published in 2020 and with the recent inflation in the number of studies focusing on the use of biomass derived CQDs in remediation and ecofriendly packaging as seen from a bibliometric analysis conducted over a period of 10 years from 2013–2023, there is a need for an extensive and comprehensive literature review on the topic. The number of biomass derived CQD related publications per year has been presented in Fig. 1(a) while Fig. 1(b) gives a comparison of this number to the total number of CQD related publications per year. The pervasive environmental pollution and the absolute need for biodegradable food packaging materials also call for literature reviews that give a better understanding of the current state and recent advances in research focused on the development of low cost, green tools for building a sustainable future. This review thus aims to give a detailed picture of the recent advancements in this area. This review presents in detail the synthesis, characterisation, surface modification, and applications of biomass derived CQDs in environmental remediation, with more focus on water treatment and food packaging applications. The detection of all the major environmental pollutants with the probable sensing mechanisms for the same and improvements in the properties of biopolymer derived packaging materials using biomass derived CQDs have been discussed. The associated challenges and the future direction for research and advancements in the applications of CQDs are also covered.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterisation of biomass derived CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: CQDs are synthesized by the carbonization of different carbon containing materials. When synthesized using this strategy, the CQDs possess widely variable but less controllable optical properties [11]. A variety of carbon rich precursors have been used for the synthesis of CQDs like chitosan [62], fruit peels [63,64], plant leaves [65,66], flowers [67], lentils [68] and other agricultural residues. CQDs can be synthesized by both top-down and bottom-up methods. Compared to top-down methods that involve the breakage of carbon containing precursors, bottom-up approaches in which smaller molecules are assembled are superior for the synthesis of CQDs. Bottom-up methods are more commonly employed for the synthesis of CQD in research as it is a low-cost synthesis approach that can be used on a large scale while also offering control over the properties of the CQDs. However harsh reaction conditions like high temperatures/alkaline conditions are required for the synthesis through bottom-up approaches [69]. For the top-down method of synthesis of CQDs, large carbon structures like CNTs and graphite are used as the precursors. These will either be subjected to chemical oxidation or different physical processes like crushing, cutting etc. to obtain the smaller CQDs. The top-down methods include laser ablation, chemical oxidation, arc discharge and electrochemical synthesis while the bottom-up methods include microwave-assisted synthesis, microplasma-assisted synthesis, ultrasonication, hydrothermal and solvothermal synthesis as shown in Fig. 2. Among these, the methods more suitable for the synthesis of CQDs from biomass sources are microwave assisted synthesis, hydrothermal synthesis, solvothermal synthesis and chemical oxidation. In addition to the method used, the precursor selected for the synthesis of the CQD also plays an important role in determining the properties of the prepared CQD as different impurities like sulphur and nitrogen present in these precursors can improve the properties of the CQD [29].

Hydrothermal/solvothermal synthesis is the most widely used method for the synthesis of CQDs from a variety of precursors. In comparison to the other bottom-up methods, hydrothermal method of synthesis requires milder reaction conditions. It is a low cost and easy, green route for the synthesis of CQDs [35]. A variety of biomass sources can be used as the precursors for the synthesis of CQDs through hydrothermal synthesis. Using hydrothermal synthesis, CQDs were synthesised from chitosan. For this purpose, ascorbic acid solution in water and chitosan dissolved in acetic acid were mixed. This was then transferred to a stainless-steel reactor and kept in the oven for 12 h at 180 °C followed by centrifugation and filtration. The CQDs were then collected through freeze drying of the filtrate. The characterisation of the CQDs was carried out by performing TEM, FT-IR, UV-Vis and fluorescence spectroscopy. The spherical shape and size of the CQDs were revealed by TEM. The functional groups present on the surface of the CQDs were verified by FT-IR to be -COOH, -NH2 and -OH. Cytotoxicity studies were also conducted to study the biocompatibility of the synthesized CQDs [62]. Mango peels were also used as a source for the synthesis of CQDs. For the hydrothermal treatment, the mango peel and water were transferred to an autoclave and heated for 4 h at 200 °C. The temperature and reaction duration seemed to directly affect the fluorescence intensity of the CQDs. With increase in the temperature from 140 to 220 °C, the intensity of the fluorescence increased. However, a temperature higher than 200 °C might increase the chances of risk in hydrothermal synthesis. When the duration of synthesis was increased, the fluorescence intensity also increased but remained gentle when the reaction was kept for longer than 4 h. From the XRD results it could be seen that the CQDs were amorphous in nature. The AFM revealed the thickness of the CQDs to be 4–7 nm [63]. Hydrothermal method of synthesis has also been used for the synthesis of nitrogen doped CQDs from empty fruit bunches (EFB). EFB powder was dissolved in deionized water and heated for 1 h till 70 °C. To this solution, urea was added and then transferred to the autoclave. It was then treated for 8 h at 180 °C. The surface morphology of the synthesized CQDs were determined using SEM, revealing a spherical morphology and uniform distribution of the CQDs as seen from the Fig. 3(a). The TEM results of the CQDs helped determine a narrow particle size distribution range as shown in Fig. 3(b) and (c). In order to analyse the crystallinity of the CQDs, XRD was performed and the CQDs were found to be highly crystalline in nature. UV-Vis and PL spectroscopy were also carried out to study the optical properties of the CQDs [64]. Nitrogen doped CQDs (N-CQDs) have also been prepared from red lentils through the hydrothermal approach. For this purpose, a solution of red lentils powder in water was transferred to the autoclave for hydrothermal synthesis at 200 °C for a duration of 5 h. The spherical shape and small size of the particles were determined using TEM. The surface groups were analysed using FT-IR and XPS. The XRD revealed the amorphous nature of the CQDs and EDS determined the elemental composition of the N-CQDs, confirming N doping [68]. For the synthesis of CQDs from the fruits of the plant Cordia myxa L. a solution of its powder was made in deionized water. The hydrothermal synthesis was then carried out at 180 °C for 4 h following which centrifugation and filtration were done to purify the CQDs. The synthesized CQDs were amorphous in nature as confirmed by the XRD results. The composition of the CQDs were determined using EDX and was found to be majorly composed of C, O and N. The high solubility of the CQDs were attributed to the presence of negatively charged groups on the surface of the CQDs as confirmed by the zeta potential value [70].

Through a one pot hydrothermal synthesis route, carbon dots (CDs) were synthesized from Couroupita guianensis fruit commonly known as canon ball (CB) fruit. To 20 mL of CB fruit juice, 30 mL of distilled water was added. Hydrothermal treatment was then carried out in an autoclave at 200 °C for a duration of 5 h. The solution turned brown-yellow and was then subjected to centrifugation and filtration. The TEM analysis reported a spherical morphology for the CDs and DLS analysis showed that the average particle size for the CDs was around 11.2 nm [71]. Grape leaves derived CDs were prepared by hydrothermal synthesis at 180 °C for 5 h. Centrifugation and filtration were performed on the obtained brown solution after which it was freeze dried. The size of the CDs was found to be in the range of 4.6 ± 0.63 nm through DLS. The CDs were found to be spherical in shape from the TEM images. The fluorescence spectra for the CDs showed excitation dependent emission and this slight shift in the fluorescence peak indicated a narrow distribution of the size of the particles [65]. Another study also reported the use of grape skin for the synthesis of CQDs (ILB-CQDs) wherein ionic liquid was used as the modifier [72]. Rajapandi et al., synthesized N doped CDs (N-CDs) from Opuntia ficus Indica fruit through the hydrothermal route. Flesh of the fruit was mixed in distilled water and placed in the oven for 8 h at 200 °C. After filtration, the yellowish white solution was stored in 4 °C. The average diameter of the N-CDs was determined to be 4.76 ± 0.91 nm from the HRTEM images. The chemical composition was studied using XPS while the FTIR helped confirm the nitrogen functionalities for the N-CDs [73]. Another study reported the hydrothermal synthesis of CQDs from acorn cups. The crushed acorn cups were mixed with deionized water and heated for 8 h at 200 °C. The solution was centrifuged after filtration and the supernatant was collected and stored. TEM results revealed evenly distributed spherical nanoparticles [74]. Pear juice derived CQDs were synthesized using hydrothermal synthesis for metal detection. Thermolysis of the pear juice was done for 36 h at 180 °C. When the morphology of the CQD was studied using TEM, nearly monodispersed, spherical CQDs were observed. The elemental composition studied by XPS showed 71 % C and 29 % O. The FTIR results revealed negatively charged functional groups responsible for giving the CQDs their characteristic PL properties [75]. The endosperm of Borassus flabellifer commonly known as ice apple was also used for CQD synthesis for heavy metal sensing applications. Juice was made out of the endosperm of the fruit and mixed with water before transferring it to an autoclave and heating at 180 °C for 12 h. The brownish-yellow solution was filtered and stored [76]. Hydrothermal method is thus one of the most widely used method because it is a simple, one pot, low cost, non-toxic and environmentally friendly synthesis route. The major issue associated with the use of hydrothermal method for CQD synthesis is the low yield as there are large amounts of hydrochar produced. More advanced synthetic routes thus use the hydrochar for the synthesis of CQDs through oxidation. Another limitation of hydrothermal synthesis is the incomplete carbonization. Top-down and bottom-up approaches have been combined to generate two step synthetic routes to overcome these challenges and hydrothermal method together with oxidation is one of the more superior methods for CQD synthesis [35].

Microwave assisted methods offer fast synthesis of CQDs at low temperatures as it enables the acceleration of chemical reactions. In addition to the shortened reaction duration, the homogenous heating will produce CQDs with uniform size distribution. The CQDs produced through microwave assisted synthesis also tend to have good quantum yield. From banana peels, CDs were prepared by microwave assisted method within 5 min at 700 W. These CDs were used for the conversion of Ag+ ions to AgNPs. The characterisation techniques used were FTIR, TEM, AFM and UV-Vis spectroscopy. The spherical shape of the CDs was revealed from the AFM images. The different functional groups present on the surface of the CDs as seen from the FTIR spectra, were responsible for the stabilization of the AgNPs. This silver nanoparticle-carbon dot assembly (AgNP@CD) can be used for the detection of blood glucose levels [77]. Spirulina platensis is a cyanobacteria from which CQDs can be synthesized for applications including dye degradation. For the microwave assisted synthesis of CQDs, a slurry of Spirulina powder was made in double distilled water. Following centrifugation, the supernatant was diluted using double distilled water. Microwave assisted synthesis was then performed at 800 W for 60 min. The characterization of the synthesized CQDs was performed using UV-Vis spectroscopy, TEM, SEM, XRD, XPS and EDX. The highly crystalline nature and high carbon content of the CQDs were confirmed by the XRD and XPS results respectively [78]. CDs with a high quantum yield of 19 % was synthesized using lotus root (LR) through a one-pot microwave assisted synthesis. LR was chopped and stirred in water. The microwave treatment was done at 800 W for 6 min following which it was centrifuged, filtered and dialyzed. The synthesized LR-CDs were used for heavy metal detection. The morphological characterization of the LR-CDs was performed using TEM and the images showed spherical particles in the size range of 5–15 nm with a diameter of 9.41 nm. The XPS spectrum revealed the C, N and O contents to be 61.81 %, 5.23 % and 32.96 %, respectively. The FTIR data indicated the presence of the functional groups -NH2, -COOH and -OH that helped increase the hydrophilicity of the LR-CDs providing them better stability in aqueous samples enabling their use in sensing applications in water [79]. Cashew gum was also used for the synthesis of CQDs through microwave assisted method. An aqueous solution of the gum was made and kept in the microwave for 30–40 min at 800 W. A brown solid was formed after carbonization. The solid was again dissolved in water and centrifuged following which it was freeze dried to obtain a C-dot composite. It showed blue emission under UV irradiation The CQDs were found to be spherical with a diameter of 9 ± 3 nm from the EFTEM images. The polysaccharide content was revealed by GPC and confirmed that the synthesized material was in fact a composite with a high polysaccharide content of 89.7 %. The FTIR results showed peaks for functional groups like -OH, -CO and -COOH that improved the hydrophilicity and stability of the CQDs [80]. Microwave assisted synthesis thus offers an easy and fast synthesis route with good control over the size of the particles. The only limitation associated with the use of this approach for CQD synthesis is the high energy cost [29].

An effective synthesis method that offers high yield under mild conditions, without large amounts of waste generation is oxidation. It involves carbonization of the carbon rich precursors, followed by their extraction using suitable oxidizing agents [81–83]. Biomass- derived CQDs were synthesized using mild oxidation with NaOH/H2O2 solution. A high yield of 76.9 wt% was obtained using this method and the synthesized CQDs had uniform size and high quantum yield. TEM and AFM revealed the structure of the CQDs. They were found to have a quasi-spherical morphology with diameter ranging from 1.5 to 4 nm. FTIR indicated the presence of epoxy, hydroxyl and carbonyl groups that were responsible for the increase in the hydrophilicity of the CQDs and subsequently their increased stability in aqueous solutions [81]. Activated carbon (AC) can serve as an ideal carbon source for CQD synthesis due to the abundance of graphite components. From AC, oxidized CQDs could be extracted using chemical oxidation. Dried AC powder was refluxed in HNO3. It was then cooled and neutralized with NaOH, followed by centrifugation, dialysis and ultrafiltration to separate the CQDs. The size of the prepared CQDs were distributed in the range of 3–4 nm as seen from the HRTEM image. The elemental analysis revealed a high C and O content of 50.01 % and 47.31 % respectively. The XPS results indicated the abundance of -COOH groups on the surface of the CQDs [82]. Hydrochar produced from food waste through hydrothermal carbonization was used for the synthesis of CDs through oxidation. For this purpose, the hydrochar was refluxed in concentrated acid following which it was subjected to dialysis. CDs with 4 different colour emissions could be obtained through this method. For the green and yellow CDs, the sizes varied in the range of 2–12 nm as seen from the TEM image. The average diameter of the red and blue CDs was 6.26 nm and 5 nm respectively [83]. Chemical oxidation is suitable for the large-scale production of CQDs and does not require high end equipment. However, the long duration of synthesis and the need for different toxic reagents are the two major drawbacks associated with this method of synthesis [29].

Ultrasonic synthesis is one of the most promising methods for the preparation of CQDs. Ultrasonication provides the advantages of higher yield, low cost and shorter reaction duration. In a simple, environment friendly synthesis approach, Park et al., prepared green CQDs (G-dots) from FW. A solution of the FW was made in ethanol and treated with ultrasound at 40 kHz for 45 min. The solution was centrifuged and filtered to remove the large particles. The AFM and TEM images showed spherical particles with an average size of 4.6 nm. FTIR along with XPS confirmed the presence of functional groups that improve the stability of the G-dots in aqueous systems enabling the use of the G-dots for different biomedical applications [84]. Hydrogen peroxide assisted ultrasonic treatment was used for the synthesis of fluorescent carbon nanoparticles (FCNPs) from AC. A suspension of AC was made in hydrogen peroxide and treated with ultrasound at 40 kHz for 2 h. The filtered solution was then purified further through the evaporation of hydrogen peroxide, then redissolving in deionized water and refluxing for 4 h at 160 °C. From the TEM image, the size of the particles was found to be in the range of 5–10 nm. The optical properties of the particles were studied using fluorescence spectroscopy and the presence of the hydrophilic functional groups on the surface were confirmed by FTIR [85]. Ultrasonication is thus a high yield, ecofriendly method for CQD synthesis. Similar to microwave assisted synthesis, high energy cost is a disadvantage of ultrasonication as well [29].

A simple and ultrafast method for the synthesis of CQDs is laser ablation. Laser ablation allows the preparation of nanoparticles from larger materials without leaving any residues thus helping in reducing contamination. A study reported the use of laser ablation for the synthesis of CQDs from carbon cloth. A dual beam laser was used for this purpose and this ablation was carried out for 10 min. The CQDs prepared using this method had a quantum yield as high as 35.4 %. Field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) was used for studying the elemental composition as well as the surface morphology. These CQDs were used for bioimaging of cells [86]. Laser ablation was also used for the synthesis of CQDs from graphitic microparticles. This work carried out the ablation of the graphitic microparticles in water and not organic solvents as the use of organic solvents might have hindered the use of CQDs in in-vivo biomedical applications. The surface functional groups were studied using FT-IR. The zeta potential value was determined to be −13 mV. Thus, the stability of the CQDs in water could be explained as the similar surface charges preventing the agglomeration of CQDs. From the TEM results, it could be seen that both the size and the size distribution of the CQDs decreased with an increase in the duration of laser irradiation. It also revealed the polycrystalline nature of the CQDs. The XRD data also showed the highly crystalline nature of the CQDs [87]. This method offers good control over the size and morphology of the CQD. But this is outweighed by the complicated nature, low yield and high cost of the method. Laser ablation is not very suitable for large scale synthesis and is also not yet commonly used for the preparation of CQDs from biomass resources [29].

Plasma is the fourth state of matter and under room temperature and atmospheric pressure, is made up of micro-meter sized plasma channels. It is therefore referred to as micro-plasma [88]. Micro-plasma assisted methods have been used for the synthesis of CQDs from different precursors like fructose, glucose, folic acid, isopropanol and citric acid [89–93]. The CDs were synthesized at atmospheric pressure using an Ar micro-plasma. Solution of isopropanol in deionized water was treated using the micro-plasma for 20 min till the solution turned yellowish. Following this the solution was filtered, dried and then dissolved in deionized water to obtain the CDs solution. XPS, UV-Vis and PL spectroscopy data were taken using this solution while the freeze-dried powder was used for TEM, FTIR, XRD and Raman analysis [89]. Tyutrin et al., subjected glucose solution to micro-plasma treatment to synthesize CQDs with luminescent properties. The synthesis was carried out at room temperature at atmospheric pressure. The solution was taken in a cuvette into which a graphite rod was immersed. At the other end of the cuvette, a stainless-steel electrode was placed right above the solution. On the steel electrode, a micro-plasma was formed using a high voltage source. The concentration of the CQDs can be controlled by varying the duration of the micro-plasma treatment. The samples were then filtered. A time-resolved confocal scanning microscope was used for studying the samples [90]. This method can produce particles with uniform size distribution and can be scaled up enabling the production on a large scale. However, the need for expensive equipment and the long duration required for the synthesis of the CQDs limit the widespread usage of this method [29].

The size, shape, morphology and quantum yield of CQDs synthesized using different synthesis routes from a variety of precursors have been summarized in Table 2. The CQDs presented spherical or quasi-spherical morphology with the average size falling in the range of 1–10 nm. The quantum yield of the fabricated CQDs, however seemed to highly vary with change in the precursors, synthesis method and reaction conditions, with quantum yields as high as 68 % and 59.31 % for certain biopolymer sources like chitosan, κ-carrageenan and agarose [94,95].

Surface modification is an important technique widely used to modify the surface properties of the nanomaterials, making them better suited for the required application. The surface modification of CQDs can be done in different ways and this includes chemical and physical modifications that alter the physicochemical properties, improve the scalability and reproducibility of the CQDs. Through surface modification, the CQDs can have well defined structure and properties, making the process of synthesis of CQDs more precise. The chemical methods used include heteroatom doping, surface passivation and functionalization. Doping with heteroatoms is one of the most commonly used method to enhance the solubility and other properties of the CQDs. The heteroatoms contain lone pair of electrons that can be used for the addition of active sites in the CQDs. Through heteroatom doping, new energy levels can be introduced in the CQD by modifying the bandgap. As a result, the electron distribution in the core and shell of the CQDs will be altered, changing the surface structure and properties [124,125]. Nitrogen atoms are the most common heteroatom dopants and for N doping of the CQDs urea, aqueous ammonia, ethylene diamine etc. are used [126]. The presence of nitrogen can increase the photocatalytic activity by enhancing the band gap of the CQDs for applications like dye degradation [73]. The photo-generated electrons are trapped by the N-CQDs, promoting the electron-hole pair separation. This, along with the large surface area of the N-CQDs improve the UV light absorption by the CQDs [127,128]. Co-doping with nitrogen and phosphorous has also been reported to improve the quantum yield of CQDs in addition to enhancement in photoluminescent properties [129]. Sulphur doping of CQDs done using reagents like sodium thiosulphate pentahydrate showed an increased number of active sites on their surface which help prevent the electron-hole pair recombination during photocatalysis. The photocatalytic activity can also be enhanced through accelerated electron transport rate due to the sulphur doping [130,131]. Bismuth nitrate pentahydrate was used as a reagent for bismuth doping and the Bi doped CQDs show higher PL intensity [132]. Surface passivation has to be performed to lower the effect of different contaminants on the properties of the CQD. Through surface passivation, the quantum yield of the CQDs can be increased. Ionic liquids have also been recently studied for the surface functionalization of CQDs. There seems to be an influence on the size of the CQDs with the change in the cationic part of the ILs while the anionic part doesn’t significantly affect the CQDs properties. ILs have high solubility and can be used in reactions to accelerate the decomposition of the raw materials, decreasing the duration of the reaction. The use of ILs can increase the quantum yield of the prepared CQDs [72,133,134]. The physical modification involves making composites of the CQDs with polymers and other nanomaterials [125]. Molecularly Imprinted Polymers (MIPs), when combined with CQDs can raise the selectivity of CQDs for sensing applications. In order to prepare MIPs, an appropriate monomer is copolymerized with a cross-linking agent in the presence of a template which will be then removed to generate sites for selective recognition. The encapsulation of the CQDs in the MIPs is done using the sol-gel technology [63]. The use of such composites for practical applications is however limited by their high cost [63,135,136].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Environmental remediation using biomass derived CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Developing a systematic solution to environmental pollution has become the need of the hour. Since the past few years, nanomaterials have been successfully developed and applied for the same. Carbon nanomaterials like CNTs, CNSs, are widely studied for environmental applications. However, compared to these materials, CQDs possess superior properties like good dispersibility in water and organic solvents owing to their surface functional groups, unique and tunable optical properties and photostability. They also exhibit different intermolecular interactions enabling their use in sensing and degradation of different environmental pollutants [26–29]. Owing to these properties of CQDs, they have gained a lot of attention in the recent past for different environmental applications as represented in Fig. 4.

The fast pace industrialization and population growth have led to the contamination of water resources globally. The release of untreated industrial effluents and sewage leads to the buildup of several toxic contaminants in water. These contaminants maybe organic, inorganic, physical, chemical, biological or radiological. This increased water pollution has created a huge disparity between the demand for water and the freshwater actually available for use. It is therefore important to carry out the treatment of contaminated water to overcome this disparity and ensure the availability of clean and fresh water for uses ranging from domestic to industrial. The conventional methods for water remediation include membrane filtration, chemical precipitation, ion-exchange, electrochemical methods and coagulation floatation. Some of these do not ensure complete purification of the waste water and may also not be cost-effective. In the recent past, nanotechnology has gained a lot of attention for potential application in waste water remediation. Among the nanomaterials that can be used for the treatment of waste water, CQDs are widely studied for their good water solubility and highly selective sensing abilities. CQDs can perform efficient detection and degradation of dyes and pharmaceuticals. They are also used in membranes to carry out water purification through forward osmosis.

Dyes released from various industries make up a major category of water pollutants. It is a tedious process to carry out the degradation of such chemically stable, cytotoxic, organic dyes. Photocatalysis is one of the most attractive methods for dye degradation due to its efficiency, low cost and sustainable nature and can be used for the effective control of environmental pollution. The most commonly used photocatalysts are TiO2 and ZnO nanoparticles. But they undergo photoexcitation only under UV irradiation [137,138]. In order to fully utilize the visible light, photocatalysts with high photoactivity and broad spectral response need to be developed. To improve the photocatalytic activity, many semiconductor materials including ZnO, CdS, C60 and CQDs are used. Semiconductor based photocatalysis has gained attention in the recent years due to their efficient photocatalytic ability, environmental friendliness and cost-effectiveness. CQDs can effectively improve the performance of the photocatalysts by increasing the range of photo-response of the catalyst and by increasing their charge separation and migration [139].

CQDs synthesized from Cordia myxa fruit were studied for its photocatalytic activity. This was tested out by using the CQDs for the degradation of four different organic dyes, methylene blue (MB), crystal violet (CV), methyl orange (MO), and Eriochrome black T (EBT). The degradation of the four dyes under different durations of UV irradiation is shown in Fig. 5. The dye that was degraded in the shortest amount of time was EBT which was done under 45 min of UV irradiation while the complete degradation of MB was carried out in less than 60 min of UV irradiation. For MO and CV, 60 min of irradiation was necessary to perform complete removal of the dyes [70]. Pear juice derived CQDs have also been tested for the degradation of MB dye. The CQDs that were synthesized through solvothermal approach showed good photocatalytic activity and was able to perform ⁓99.5 % degradation of the dye in 130 min [75]. Spirulina platensis derived CQDs were studied for the degradation of the textile dye Red M8B. For a concentration of 200 ppm, 6 h was the time taken by the CQDs for complete dye degradation. Above this concentration, the time required for complete dye degradation exceeded 6 h. Following the dye degradation, the CQDs could be removed from the water sample using charcoal [78]. A study was also conducted using CQDs synthesized from Ocimum basilicum seeds. The CQDs showed good photocatalytic activity with dye degradation within a short duration [140]. Another study reported the use of Coccinia grandis fruit extract for the synthesis of N-CDs for MO reduction. When compared to the reduction of the dye using NaBH4, the addition of N-CDs further accelerated the reduction of the dye. The reaction using this green catalyst was found to be kinetically and thermodynamically favoured [141]. Spent coffee grounds (SCG) were also used as the carbon source for the synthesis of CQDs for application in dye degradation. The NCQDs were then used for decorating TiO2 for MB degradation. The mechanism for dye degradation with the synthesized NCQDs is illustrated in Fig. 6. Under solar irradiation, the electrons in the valence band (VB) of TiO2 absorb photons, leading to excitation of the electron (e-) hole (h+) pairs. But the e- and h+ easily recombine at low activity. The utilization of NCQDs has the potential to enhance visible light harvesting and promote charge transfer. Consequently, a greater number of photocarriers are produced and their recombination is significantly inhibited, resulting in a notable rise in effective active radicals. The use of 3 wt% NCQDs increased the reaction rate by 2.25 times. Even after 4 cycles, the photodegradation rate was as high as 86 % [142]. NCQDs synthesized from Cucurbita pepo fruit extract through hydrothermal synthesis was studied for the degradation of CV. When the photocatalytic activity of the dots was analysed, it could be seen that maximum dye degradation could be achieved when 100 mg CQDs were used with 1 mL H2O2. [143].

Wastewater treatment using forward osmosis (FO), has drawn a lot of attention for its higher efficiency over other pressure driven technologies for water treatment. However, membrane fouling is a major issue affecting the performance of the membranes. One of the approaches used to overcome this limitation is membrane modification using different nanoparticles. Among the nanomaterials used for this purpose, CQDs can effectively prevent membrane fouling while also imparting antibacterial and antifouling properties to the membrane. CQDs from oil palm biomass when used for the modification of polysulfone membrane surface (CQDs-PSF), showed enhancement in the water flux with high concentrations of CQD. The CQDs increased the hydrophilicity and smoothness of the membrane surface which in turn helped prevent the fouling of the membrane. The modified membranes also showed good antibacterial properties [144]. Another study reported that the addition of corn stalk derived N-CQDs and polydopamine (PDA) treatment to thin film composite membrane (TFCCQD) improved the chlorine resistance and water flux of the membranes. The redesigned membrane exhibited improved chlorine resistance, the mechanism for which is schematically illustrated in Fig. 7. The N-CQDs added act as sacrificial sites that react with the chlorine free radicals, protecting the membrane and increasing the chlorine resistance [145].

CQDs have been increasingly studied for their potential in the sensing of different environmental pollutants due to the several advantages they offer. They show good photoluminescence, solubility, biocompatibility, low toxicity and are environmentally friendly [13]. There are two different mechanisms by which CQDs can be used in sensing. It can either be through the fluorescence quenching by the analyte or through the fluorescence enhancement of a quenched CQD-quencher complex. This takes place through the different intermolecular interactions between the CQD and the analytes. CQDs have been studied widely for the sensing of environmental contaminants. This includes heavy metal ions like Pb2+, Hg2+, Fe3+, Pd2+, Cr6+, Ag+ and Cu2+, antibiotics like tetracycline (TC) and amoxicillin (AMO) and many different pesticides and herbicides. There are different mechanisms that can explain the fluorescence quenching of the CQDs, static and dynamic quenching. When there is a linear change in the intensity of fluorescence with the change in the concentration of the analyte, it is said to have single dynamic or static quenching mechanism. The quencher can combine with the fluorescent molecule in to form ground state non-fluorescent complexes leading to static quenching [146,147]. If there is a non-linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity and quencher concentration, the mechanism will be a combination of the two [63]. In dynamic quenching, the fluorescent molecule in the excited state will collide with the quencher leading to electron or energy transfer. With dynamic quenching, changes will not be observed in the fluorescent molecule’s absorption spectra [148]. If there is an overlap between the absorption spectra and fluorescence spectra of the quencher and fluorescent molecule respectively, the two possible mechanisms for fluorescence quenching are Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) and inner filter effect (IFE). FRET takes place through energy transfer between CQD’s excited state and the quencher molecule’s ground state whereas with IFE, the emission will be filtered off by a second absorber leading to the excited beam getting attenuated [149]. Another possible mechanism behind fluorescent sensing by CQDs is photoinduced electron transfer (PET) wherein electrons will be photoexcited to higher energy levels and undergo recombination with the quencher molecules [150]. In order to distinguish between the different mechanisms and determine the mechanism of quenching, fluorescence life time studies are conducted.

Heavy metals are one of the most common pollutants of water resources and are highly toxic even in low doses. Their levels in the environment have seen a rise due to geogenic and industrial activities. Their accumulation in the environment can lead to several adverse health effects in humans. This includes damage to the liver, kidney and the central nervous system. This calls for efficient methods for the detection and removal of heavy metals from the water bodies. The conventional methods used for the purpose often require expensive instrumentation and expertise making on-site evaluation a challenge. In order to overcome these limitations, different nanomaterials have been reported over these years. Among those, CQDs seem to carry immense potential for sensing due to their tuneable optical properties and economic feasibility [151] A turn-off sensor for the detection of Cu2+ was developed using CQDs synthesized from Eleusine coracana by Murugan et al. The quenching of the fluorescence of the CQDs showed a linear relationship with the concentration of Cu2+ in the range of 0–100 µM. The fluorescence quenching of the CQDs in the presence of different metal ions was studied. It was seen that the relative quenching was the highest with the addition of Cu2+ ions as shown in Fig. 8(a). The strong affinity between the Cu2+ ions and CQDs can be seen from the histogram plotted by taking the relationship between the quenching ratio (F/F0) and equal concentrations of different metal ions, with Cu2+ exhibiting the lowest value in Fig. 8(b). The visual images of the dispersion of metal ions with CQDs at 365 nm is presented in Fig. 8(c)., again exhibiting the highly selective fluorescence quenching by Cu2+ ions [152]. A dual “off-on” sensor based on CQDs synthesized from Prosopis juliflora leaves was employed in the detection of Hg2+ ions and the drug chemet used for the anti-poisoning of heavy metal ions [66]. CQDs derived from several other sources of biomass waste have also been studied for the detection of Fe3+ and showed 94–108 % recovery when used for Fe3+ detection in pond water [67]. Using grape skin as the source and ionic liquid as the modifier, ILB-CQDs were synthesized for the detection of both Fe3+ and Pd2+ in water samples. By using ionic liquids, the reaction time could be reduced while the quantum yield showed an improvement. The detection limits were as low as 3.2 nmol/L and 0.36 µmol/L for Fe3+ and Pd2+ respectively in real water samples [72]. For the sensing of Fe3+ by fluorescence quenching mechanism, CQDs were synthesized from Borassus flabellifer endosperm and the prepared CQDs showed good water solubility and stability. It exhibited selectivity for Fe3+ ions as it was seen that all the other prepared metal ions did not show any significant decrease in the fluorescence intensity of the CQD [76].

The global population explosion fuels an ever-increasing demand for food which leads to an increased use of different pesticides and herbicides. Many of these, while being effective in performing their role, do tend to accumulate and pollute the environment over time, adversely affecting the ecosystem. The conventional methods like chromatography, mass spectrometry and electrochemical techniques used for the detection of pesticides are complicated and require skilled technicians to operate the sophisticated instruments for the same [153]. There is a need for simpler, cost-effective methods that enable rapid, on-site detection of the pesticides. Fluorescence based sensors are potential alternatives that serve all of these above-mentioned functions while also being highly selective and sensitive [154]. CQDs are good candidates for this purpose and can interact with different pesticides leading to enhancement or quenching of the fluorescence intensity of the CQDs. CQDs synthesized from gelatin through hydrothermal synthesis showed fluorescence enhancement on interaction with imidacloprid, a neuro-active insecticide belonging to the class of neonicotinoids. It is systemic insecticide used for pest control. The same CQD however showed quenching of fluorescence with the four pesticides atrazine, chlorpyrifos, lindane, and tetradifon. The limit of detection was as low as 0.013 μM for imidacloprid and 0.04 μM for tetradifon [155]. Pretilachlor is a toxic herbicide used to control the growth of different weeds. When inhaled or consumed it can cause vertigo, headache, giddiness, eye and skin irritation in humans. CDs synthesized from water hyacinth leaves was studied for the detection of pretilachlor and performed highly selective and sensitive detection with a lower detection limit of 2.9 μM [156]. For the detection of the herbicide mesotrione, CQDs were synthesized from mango peels and encapsulated in MIPs to enhance their selectivity. This probe when used for the mesotrione detection in corn, the detection limit was found to be 4.7 nmol L−1 [63]. Fenitrothion (FNT) belonging to the group of organophosphorous pesticides is used as an insecticide for rice, fruits, vegetables etc. FNT residues in water can affect human health adversely. Sugar cane juice derived CDs were used for the detection of FNT using dispersive liquid-liquid microextraction (DLLME) method. The increase in the absorbance signal of the CDs in the presence of FNT was considered the analytical signal for FNT and the lower detection limit of FNT was 0.2 ng mL−1 [157]. Another study reported the use of Brassica oleracea juice derived CDs for the detection of the pesticides diazinon, glyphosate and amicarbazone through the fluorescence quenching of the CDs. The detection limit for diazinon, glyphosate and amicarbazone were 0.252 ng mL−1, 0.52 ng mL−1 and 2 ng mL−1 respectively [158].

Nitroaromatic compounds like nitrophenols (NPs) are toxic organic pollutants that are harmful to human health adverse impacts on liver, kidney and the central nervous system [159]. They are toxic environmental pollutants whose removal and degradation from water sources is challenging due to their high stability and solubility. The traditional methods used for the detection and degradation of these compounds are complex and time consuming, calling for more simple, accurate and efficient detection methods. CQDs have been prepared and studied for the same. CDs synthesized through hydrothermal synthesis from celery leaves showed blue fluorescence and were used for the development of paper sensors for detection of NPs. The detection of 2-NP, 3-NP and 4-NP was carried out with good selectivity and sensitivity by the CQDs [102]. Trinitrophenol (TNP) detection was performed using CDs derived from Abelmoschus manihot flowers. The fluorescence intensity of the CDs with strong blue emission was quenched sensitively and selectively by TNP in real water samples [103]. Rosa roxburghii fruit was used as the carbon source for the preparation of CQDs with blue fluorescence for the detection of o-nitrophenol (O-NP) in water samples. The very low detection limit indicates that the synthesized CQDs could be used as fluorescent probes for detection of O-NP [104].

Hypochlorite is used for water treatment due to their germicidal properties. However, according to the guidelines of United States Environmental Protection Agency (USEPA), the concentration of hypochlorite in water should not be exceeding a maximum of 4 mg/L. Fluorometric sensors have recently become popular for the detection of hypochlorite in water. CDs synthesized from Brassica compestris L. were used for the detection of hypochlorite in tap water and successful detection of hypochlorite could be performed with detection limits as low as 0.015 μM. In addition to hypochlorite detection, the CDs were also used for detection of Hg2+ [106]. Prickly pear cactus was another source of CDs synthesized for the detection of hypochlorite and As3+ ions. Selective fluorescence quenching of the CDs took place in the presence of hypochlorite and As3+ ions over other metal ions [107]. Sweet pepper derived CQDs were used for carrying out hypochlorite detection with high selectivity and a fast response time [105].

A wide variety of antimicrobial agents are used in day-to-day life for the prevention and treatment of several diseases. Most of these are highly toxic and can cause grave health issues. The overuse of antibiotics has led to environmental pollution and the very serious concern of development of multidrug resistant strains of bacteria which would render the antibiotics useless for further applications in disease treatment. The most commonly overused antibiotic is TC and the presence of this antibiotic is frequently detected in water and soil samples. This can further lead to the accumulation of the antibiotic in the human body with serious side effects on human health. This calls for detection methods that are simple and risk free and can be used for on-site detection [160]. TC detection was studied using fluorescence quenching of the CDs prepared from fish roe using hydrothermal synthesis through the mechanism of IFE as shown in Fig. 9. The CDs showed good selectivity and specificity for TC. The limit of detection by the CDs was 41.7 nM and TC detection was carried out in milk, serum and river water with recovery rates as high as 98.6–104.5 % [161]. Curcuma amada was used as the carbon source for synthesis of CDs for detection of the antibiotic fluoroquinolones and TCs. The detection limits were 33 nM and 2 nM for TC and fluoroquinolones respectively [149]. Durio zibethinus or durian shell waste was also used as a source for the detection of TC. The N-CDs synthesized using the hydrothermal method exhibited selective detection of TC through fluorescence quenching of N-CDs due to the IFE, with a detection limit of 75 nM [162]. AMO is a broad-spectrum antibiotic used for treating different bacterial infections in humans and animals. It is an effective antibiotic but is an allergen, toxic and environmental pollutant. CQDs synthesized from beet root powder were used as sensors for the detection of AMO. With increase in the concentration of AMO, the fluorescence intensity also showed increase and the lower limit of detection was 0.475 μM [163]

CQDs offer several advantages and detection strategies for use in sensing applications. The different fluorescence sensing mechanisms of biomass derived CQDs are summarized in Table 3. The surface functional groups of the CQD are heavily responsible for the mechanism of fluorescence quenching of the CQDs. The presence of hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on the surface of the CQD makes it sensitive to different metal ions and organic pollutants. Static quenching is reported to be the most probable sensing mechanism for metal ions, often caused by the aggregation of CQDs, due to the strong affinity between metal ions and the surface functional groups of the CQDs [67,94]. A spectral overlapping between the CQD and the quencher molecule is indicative of two possible mechanisms, IFE and FRET. Most of the studies have employed fluorescence lifetime studies to differentiate between the two. For FRET, the fluorescence lifetime shows a change whereas for IFE, the fluorescence lifetime change is negligible [149,164]. Babu et al., used Time-correlated single-photon counting (TCSPC) studies to confirm FRET as the mechanism for the fluorescence quenching by lead ions [21]. Surface doping also affected the quenching mechanism as nitrogen and oxygen rich surfaces of the doped CQDs facilitate electron transfer, proposing a FRET mechanism for fluorescence quenching [111] . Dynamic quenching, which is fluorescence quenching caused by electron transfer from the CQDs to the metal ions has also been reported [165]. Even with the reported mechanisms, there seems to be a lack of clarity in the proposed fluorescence quenching mechanisms of CQDs by different environmental pollutants and therefore needs to be studied in detail. Their high solubility, surface area and high intensity fluorescence along, tunable properties and the different intermolecular interactions with the analyte make them befitting for sensing applications. However, the major limitation in using CQDs as sensing probes is their low specificity in comparison to the traditional methods. This calls for additional purification steps and surface modifications of the CQD. There needs to be more precise synthesis methods offering better control over the size, shape and properties of the CQDs [13].

Adsorption is one of the most versatile, efficient and economic methods for the removal of toxic environmental pollutants like heavy metals from water resources. Different carbonaceous materials like AC, CNTs, GQDs and biochar are widely studied for this application. Among the different adsorbents used for this purpose, CQDs offer easy and inexpensive synthesis from different agricultural sources and is also visually observable as an adsorbent. A composite of CQDs with CNTs (CQD-CNT) were developed and the adsorption of MB dye by CQD-CNT was higher due to the larger surface area, surface functional groups and pore structures [170]. For the adsorption of Pb2+ ions, CQDs were prepared from various cellulosic materials through microwave heating. The removal efficiency was 99.16 % for the CQD synthesized from bagasse and was the highest among the different cellulosic CQDs [171]. Pudza et. al., prepared CDs from tapioca flour for heavy metal adsorption. The removal efficiency was as high as 80.6 % with a contact time of 260 min. The optimum pH range for the adsorption of Pb2+ ions was found to be from 7–13. This can be explained by the zeta potential value of the CDs at pH 7 as the zeta potential value was −31.7 mV at pH 7, indicating the presence of negative surface charges that enable Pb2+ adsorption [172]. The adsorption and removal of heavy metal ions by CQDs thus seem to take place through chemisorption between the heavy metal ions and different functional groups on the surface of the CQD. Table 4 summarizes the precursors, synthetic route and application of biomass derived CQD for environmental remediation.

Maintaining the freshness of fruits and vegetables and preventing food spoilage before it reaches the consumers is a difficult task. What makes this even more concerning is the non- biodegradability of the commonly used petroleum derived packaging materials and the huge amounts of waste generation due to the same. This calls for the development of packaging materials that are biodegradable while offering other favourable conditions to maintain the freshness of food articles. Biopolymers have drawn a lot of attention for the development of such eco-friendly packaging materials. Despite their environment friendliness, there are certain drawbacks to the large-scale use of biopolymer nanocomposites. The thermal, mechanical and barrier properties of these materials do not seem to be comparable with the conventional petroleum derived packaging materials. The high cost and other complexities associated with the production of these also hold back industries from using these. In order to overcome some of these difficulties and impart favourable properties to biopolymer packaging films, different nanofillers are introduced into the polymer matrices. Nanofillers have their sizes in the nanoscale and can improve the mechanical, barrier, antimicrobial and antioxidant properties of the polymer composites used in food packaging. With the recent focus on nanofillers to improve the properties of biopolymer films, CQDs have garnered attention for their antimicrobial, antioxidant and UV shielding properties [203,204]. When added as nanofillers to a polymer matrix, CQDs have also been reported to improve the barrier and mechanical properties of the polymer composites. The use of CQDs as nanofillers and the enhancement of the different properties of packaging films is schematically represented in Fig. 10. Their good compatibility with the different biopolymers and low cytotoxicity makes them an ideal and sustainable nanofiller for packaging applications.

In order to prepare CQD incorporated biopolymer films, two techniques can be followed. The CQDs can either be coated on the film or they can be added into the solution directly during the solution casting. Coating method was used for the incorporation of mulberry extract derived C-dots on bacterial nanocellulose (BNC) as this is a method ideal for highly porous polymers with good capacity for adsorption [205]. If solution casting is followed, it is important to optimize the concentration of CQDs in the solution as it may undergo aggregation at high concentrations. As the CQDs undergo aggregation, addition of CQDs at high concentrations to the biopolymer films tends to have adverse effects on the mechanical and barrier properties of the films. The amount of CQDs incorporated into the packaging films is an important parameter that needs to be carefully considered for desirable modifications in the film properties [204,206,207]. Chitosan derived CDs were incorporated into gelatin/chitosan derived films for packaging fish meat. The film showed better antioxidant, antibacterial and mechanical properties with the increase in the concentration of the CDs. The addition of the CDs also improved the UV blocking performance of the film. The film also acted as a pH indicator to monitor the freshness of the meat [208]. In a slightly different approach, soy milk preservation was carried out using a combination of banana derived CDs with chitosan solution. The stability of soy milk at room temperature was higher and the microbial growth was also efficiently controlled [209]. Cyanobacteria are aquatic and photosynthetic bacteria that form toxic blooms in waterbodies posing a threat to different forms of aquatic life and also to humans. From cyanobacteria, CQDs were synthesized hydrothermally for use in PVA composite films in addition to nanocellulose. The CQDs seemed to improve the water, light and UV barrier properties of the films [210]

ce:section.ce:section-title: Improvement in antibacterial and antioxidant properties
ce:section.ce:para: Active food packaging should be able to prevent food spoilage through adverse biological and chemical changes in the food products due to microbial growth and oxidation. Packaging materials possessing antimicrobial and antioxidant properties are thus very desirable for the purpose. CQDs are one such category of nanomaterials which when used as fillers in packaging materials impart it with good antimicrobial and antioxidant properties. The antimicrobial property of CQD is due to the surface charges of the CQD which may lead to attractive or repulsive forces between the CQD and the bacterial cells depending on whether the CQDs possess positive or negative surface charges [203]. The CQDs can also penetrate the nanoscale pores in the bacterial walls causing the intercellular components to spill out and lead to cell death [211]. The surface groups like hydroxyl groups are once again responsible for the antioxidant activity of CQDs as they can act as hydrogen or electron donors for free radical scavenging activity [204]. Chitosan, one of the most abundant biopolymers, can be used as the precursor material for CQD synthesis as it has inherent antimicrobial and antioxidant properties. The CQDs prepared from chitosan possess antibacterial and antioxidant properties due to the functional groups like -COOH, -OH and -NH2 on the surface. CQDs prepared hydrothermally from chitosan were used as fillers in carboxymethyl cellulose films for extending the shelf life of lemon fruit. The antioxidant property of these films was analysed using ABTS and DPPH methods. While the ABTS method revealed 100 % antioxidant activity, the DPPH method showed only 87–88 % antioxidant activity attributed to the ability of the aqueous solution of ABTS to interact well with the hydrophilic CQDs while the methanolic DPPH solution cannot. The films also exhibited good antibacterial properties with lower concentrations of CQD showing bacteriostatic effect and higher concentrations of CQD exhibiting bactericidal effects [62]. Enhancement in the antibacterial properties of chitosan nanocomposite films were performed with the help of turmeric derived CQDs as schematically presented in Fig. 11. [212]. In another study, pectin films were incorporated with glucose derived CQDs. When compared to pure pectin films that exhibited only 7.2 % and 4.2 % antioxidant activity in the ABTS and DPPH studies, CQD addition increased the antioxidant activity to 95 % and 92 % respectively. Similar effects were also observed in the antimicrobial properties of the pectin films with the pure pectin films exhibiting no antimicrobial properties and CQD added pectin films showing bactericidal and fungicidal properties that increased with increase in the CQD concentration [213]. Modified atmosphere packaging (MAP) is a very effective way to maintain the freshness of food products to increase their shelf life. Laminaria japonica derived CDs incorporated chitosan films (CDs/CH) were developed and used in preservation of cucumber. The MAP was able to control microbial growth on the freshly cut cucumber. This study also reported an increase in the antibacterial properties of the film with increase in the concentration of the CDs. The firmness and flavour of the vegetable were also well retained by the MAP [214].

Oxidation and discoloration of food products when exposed to UV radiation is another major cause of food deterioration during the processing and transport of packaged food products. This directly affects the sensory experiences of the consumer and the nutritional value of the food products [215]. The packaging films must be able to prevent the photocatalytic degradation that takes place through different photochemical reactions to ensure the quality of food products for longer durations. CQDs have unique optical properties with source dependent absorption in the UV region and can perform light scattering. The light transmittance of the packaging films is modified by the addition of CQDs, as these are excellent photon harvesters, specifically in the short wavelength region due to the different electronic transitions taking place in the CQDs [216,217]. When added in packaging films, CQDs have shown to considerably increase the light absorption by the packaging films. This makes them suitable for use in packaging as UV shielding agents to efficiently prevent oxidation of food components through exposure to light. CQD addition to pectin films were reported to prevent more than 90 % UV transmission and the UV shielding property of the films increased with increase in the concentration of CQD [213]. Tea derived CDs added to PVA films were used for the preservation of grape fruit. The films showed very good UV blocking abilities without highly affecting the mechanical properties and thermal stability of the films [218]. CQDs synthesized from Pinus radiata residue were also added to PVA films. The improvement in the UV shielding by the films was attributed to the addition of CQDs. These highly transparent films also exhibited superior mechanical properties with increase in the concentration of added CQDs [219]. The increase in the UV shielding properties of the films was reported to have a direct relationship with the concentration of the CDs. This increase in UV absorption was also responsible for increase in the thermal resistance of the films [220].

Mechanical properties play a very significant role in determining the ability of the film to protect the food from external stress and strain during transport, storage and sales. The strong electrostatic interactions between the polymer matrix and the CQDs are responsible for the improvement of the mechanical properties of the films [65,220]. The hydroxyl groups present on the surface of the CQD interact and form hydrogen bonds with the carboxylic and amino groups of biopolymers as the CQD undergoes dispersion. Gelatin films strengthened using cross-linking with oxidized Persian gum (PG) was incorporated with CDs for food packaging applications. The CDs were synthesized from grape leaves through hydrothermal synthesis. Cross-linking with PG and the addition of CDs up to 5 wt% improved the TS of the gelatin films [65]. The TS of biopolymer films shows improvement on addition of optimum concentration on CQDs as higher concentrations lead to aggregation which might further lower the TS of the films. However, the effect of CQDs on the mechanical properties of the films depend on the polymer matrix, type, properties and concentrations of the added CQDs. Previous studies have also reported decrease in the TS values of films after an initial increase with CQD addition as shown in Fig. 12(a) [221]. Use of tea derived CDs as functional fillers also showed similar effects on the TS of the chitosan films as the addition of CDs initially increased the TS of the films but further with increase in the concentration of the CDs, the TS of the films decreased [222]. The increased cross-linking density due to the strong electrostatic interactions upon addition of the CQDs to a polymer matrix however may also decrease the flexibility of the films while improving the toughness of the films. This effect is also responsible for a decrease in the elongation at break (EB) values of the films. The addition of CDs to fish scale gelatin (FSG) and alginate dialdehyde (ADA) composite films exhibited decrease in the EB values with the incorporation of pomelo peel derived CDs (PCDs) most probably due to the increased hydrogen bonding between CDs and the matrix. The effect of the addition of PCDs on the mechanical properties of the films is depicted in the Fig. 12 (a)-(c) [221].

The packaging must effectively protect the food from different external environmental agents and for this reason must possess good water and gas barrier properties. The films must be able to prevent or reduce the transfer of moisture between the products and the environment [65]. A major disadvantage of biopolymers when used in packaging is their high hydrophilicity and water vapour permeability (WVP). Addition of biomass derived CQDs can bring down the WVP of the biopolymer films through the convolution of the water vapour diffusion channels in the polymer matrix. A study reported the development of pectin/gelatin composite films with CQDs derived from turmeric for food packaging. These films showed good antioxidant, antibacterial, UV shielding and mechanical properties. The water vapour permeability showed a decrease but the hydrophilicity of the film increased with the addition of the CQDs as seen from the decrease in the water contact angle. This is possibly due to the hydrophilic functional groups present on the surface of the CQDs [223]. Other studies have also reported an increase in the WVP of the films on incorporation of CQDs. The increase in the number of hydroxyl groups with the addition of CQDs results in increase in interaction with water molecules leading to higher WVP. Addition of CDs derived from SCG into gelatin/PVA films exhibited an increase in the WVP and decrease in the hydrophobicity of the films [224]. Similar effects were observed in the WVP of chitosan/starch films on addition of green tea derived CDs. The films made of chitosan/starch were already rich in free hydroxyl groups but the inclusion of CDs further increase the abundance of hydroxyl groups ultimately leading to an increase in the WVP of the films [225]. The barrier properties of FSG films were modified on addition of PCDs as shown in Fig. 12. (d)-(f) [221]. A literature survey conducted showed very few studies on the use of biomass derived CQDs for improving the properties of packaging films with relatively greater number of papers coming out after 2022. Publications discussing the effects of biomass derived CQDs on the mechanical, barrier, UV shielding, antioxidant and antimicrobial properties of the films have been summarized in Table 5.

The rapid growth of population and subsequent increase in the demand for day-to-day products challenge sustainable development due to rampant environmental pollution and energy crisis. Immediate and serious efforts have to be made for the mitigation of environmental pollution and improving the quality of life. Nanotechnology has been the point of focus for its ability to make significant contribution to environmental protection. At the forefront of the attempts to utilize nanotechnology for environmental remediation are carbon nanomaterials and among the many carbon-based nanomaterials, CQDs stand out for their unique properties. The recent studies conducted on the use of CQDs for controlling environmental pollution also suggest that CQDs carry immense potential as tools in environmental remediation. The other important application of CQDs is in the biodegradable food packaging market further promoting sustainability. The growing food industry currently is in need for sustainable, biodegradable, reusable and active packaging materials that are also durable enough to withstand the external conditions while maintaining the freshness of food. As reported in literature, the low cytotoxicity, good biocompatibility and optical properties make them suitable candidates for use as nanofillers. Their high antioxidant and antimicrobial properties also make them safe for use in active food packaging applications. Agricultural waste residues and food wastes are being increasingly used for the production of CQDs. Biomass derived CQDs negate the need for costly precursors. The different organic raw materials also make it easier to perform surface functionalization of the CQDs. The wide variety of raw materials allow the production of CQDs with different properties owing to natural functionalization of the CQD surface. Biomass derived CQD is thus one of the most sustainable and economic option among the more commonly used nanomaterials environmental pollution control and remediation. Different biomass sources, synthesis methods and applications of the CQDs have been discussed. The hydrothermal approach is the most often described methodology for synthesising CQDs from biomass sources, owing to the simplicity and low-cost nature of the same. Synthesis methods like laser ablation and micro-plasma assisted synthesis are not yet widely reported for CQD synthesis from biomass sources. Biomass derived CQDs when employed for environmental remediation, the two more frequently reported applications are photocatalytic dye degradation and heavy metal sensing. They also show good ability to perform selective detection of antibiotics and pesticides with very low detection limits. CQDs derived from different biomass sources can also be successfully incorporated into biopolymer-based films for developing active, biodegradable food packaging materials. The use of CQDs as fillers have been reported to improve the mechanical and barrier properties of the film while also giving the films antimicrobial and antioxidant properties, enabling long term storage of the food products through prevention of interaction with external environmental agents and microbial contamination. The low toxicity of biomass derived CQDs allow their use in packaging films that come in direct contact with the food products. However, there are still plenty more biomass sources that can be used for the preparation of CQDs with varying properties as suggested by the fewer number of publications on biomass derived CQDs in comparison to that of CQDs and therefore CQDs carry a lot of potential in monitoring and controlling environmental pollution, which is yet to be fully unlocked.

Even then, the large-scale usage of CQDs for the same is hindered by several issues starting from their synthesis. While CQDs can be synthesized from a variety of precursors through different top-down and bottom-up approaches, the management of the by-products have to be taken care of. Lack of purity and homogeneity is another major limitation of biomass derived CQDs. The methods of synthesis should also be offering better control over the properties of the CQDs if they are to be used for the different applications simultaneously. Even though CQDs are relatively non-toxic, it is still necessary to perform their recovery from the environment after the application and presently, studies on the ecological risks associated with the use of CQDs in the environment are also limited. Another matter to be considered is the real-world applicability of CQDs to control environmental pollution. Biopolymer-based packaging films, with CQDs as nanofillers carry immense potential for future applications in packaging. However, the commercialization of the same requires large scale production of CQDs while most of the reported studies have been limited to small scale synthesis in laboratories. The cost-effectiveness of such large-scale fabrication of CQDs and reproducibility of their properties must be thoroughly investigated. It is equally important to perform stability studies on the CQDs and the films under different conditions for extended durations if they are to be employed in the long-term storage of a variety of food products. Edible films and coatings have garnered a lot of attention recently and the use of CQDs for the development of edible films also needs to reviewed for the safety aspects. Future studies must be focused on overcoming all these challenges. With the optimisation of synthesis conditions and most importantly through surface modification strategies, CQDs can be developed for use as highly efficient, biocompatible, cost-effective, environment friendly green tools in environmental remediation and eco-friendly packaging applications.
D. Sangeetha: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition. Geethanjali Bijoy: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization.
